facts.t: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776.
Thomas Paine's pamphlet 'Common Sense,' published in January 1776, argued forcefully for independence.
British forces hanged Nathan Hale as a spy in September 1776; he reportedly regretted having only one life to give.
The Declaration of Independence was first read aloud publicly in Philadelphia on July 8, 1776.
New Yorkers toppled a gilded statue of King George III in 1776, later melting it into musket balls.
American victory at the Battles of Saratoga in October 1777 became the war's turning point.
Philadelphia held the first annual Fourth of July celebration in 1777, with bonfires and fireworks.
Congress adopted the Stars and Stripes as the national flag on June 14, 1777.
The Marquis de Lafayette, a young French aristocrat, arrived in America and volunteered for the Continental Army.
Continental Army troops began their brutal winter encampment at Valley Forge in December 1777.
France signed the Treaty of Alliance with the United States in February 1778, entering the war against Britain.
Baron von Steuben drilled ragged Continental troops at Valley Forge in 1778, forging a disciplined army.
At the Battle of Monmouth in June 1778, Mary Hays reportedly carried water to soldiers, inspiring the 'Molly Pitcher' legend.
British forces evacuated Philadelphia in June 1778 after nearly a year of occupation.
George Rogers Clark captured the British-held town of Kaskaskia in July 1778 during the frontier campaign.
John Paul Jones led USS Bonhomme Richard to a stunning win at the Battle of Flamborough Head in September 1779.
Spain declared war on Britain in June 1779, indirectly aiding the American cause.
Washington ordered the Sullivan Expedition in 1779, a campaign against Iroquois nations allied with Britain.
The Continental dollar's value collapsed in 1779, giving rise to the phrase 'not worth a Continental.'
Polish cavalry commander Casimir Pulaski died in October 1779 from wounds at the Siege of Savannah.
Benedict Arnold's plot to surrender West Point to the British was exposed in September 1780.
British forces captured Charleston, South Carolina in May 1780, the war's worst American defeat.
The brutal 'Hard Winter' of 1780 froze New York Harbor solid enough to march cannons across the ice.
Major John Andre, Arnold's British contact, was captured and hanged as a spy in October 1780.
Pennsylvania passed a gradual abolition law in 1780, the first such act among the American states.
Cornwallis surrendered to Washington and French forces at the Siege of Yorktown in October 1781.
The Articles of Confederation, America's first constitution, were ratified in March 1781.
Unpaid and exhausted, Continental soldiers of the Pennsylvania Line mutinied against Congress in January 1781.
The Bank of North America, the first chartered American bank, was established in 1781.
Spanish and American forces under Bernardo de Galvez captured Pensacola from the British in May 1781.
Britain's Parliament voted to end the war in early 1782, and Lord North's government soon collapsed.
Congress adopted the Great Seal of the United States, featuring the bald eagle, in June 1782.
George Washington created the Badge of Military Merit in 1782, a forerunner of the modern Purple Heart.
Congress authorized the Aitken Bible in 1782, the first complete English-language Bible printed in America.
The Battle of Blue Licks in Kentucky, one of the war's last major clashes, was fought in August 1782.
The Treaty of Paris, signed in September 1783, formally ended the Revolutionary War.
George Washington bade an emotional farewell to his officers at New York's Fraunces Tavern in December 1783.
The last British troops left New York City on Evacuation Day, November 25, 1783.
Noah Webster published his famous 'blue-backed speller' in 1783 to standardize American spelling.
Unpaid soldiers surrounded Congress in Philadelphia during the Pennsylvania Mutiny of 1783.
Congress formally ratified the Treaty of Paris on January 14, 1784, officially ending the war.
The Empress of China, the first American ship to trade with China, sailed from New York in 1784.
Thomas Jefferson's Land Ordinance of 1784 laid early groundwork for admitting new western states.
An early American hot-air balloon launched from Bladensburg, Maryland, thrilled onlookers in 1784.
The 1784 Treaty of Fort Stanwix redrew land boundaries between the U.S. and the Iroquois.
The Land Ordinance of 1785 created the grid survey system still used to divide much of the country.
John Adams became America's first minister to Great Britain in 1785.
Thomas Jefferson succeeded Benjamin Franklin as U.S. minister to France in 1785.
Vermont, not yet a state, began minting its own copper coins in 1785.
Benjamin Franklin returned home to Philadelphia in 1785 after years as a diplomat in France.
Massachusetts farmers crushed by debt launched the armed uprising known as Shays's Rebellion in 1786.
The Annapolis Convention of 1786 called for a broader meeting to overhaul the Articles of Confederation.
Virginia enacted Jefferson's Statute for Religious Freedom in 1786, separating church and state.
Revolutionary War general Nathanael Greene died at his Georgia plantation in June 1786, aged 43.
The Ohio Company of Associates organized in 1786 to settle the new Northwest Territory.
Delegates signed the new U.S. Constitution in Philadelphia on September 17, 1787.
The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 banned slavery in new territories and set rules for statehood.
Delaware became the first state to ratify the Constitution, on December 7, 1787.
At 81, Benjamin Franklin was the oldest delegate at the 1787 Constitutional Convention.
Delegates adopted the Three-Fifths Compromise on representation during the 1787 convention.
New Hampshire's ratification in June 1788 made the new Constitution officially operative.
Hamilton, Madison, and Jay published the Federalist Papers in 1787-88 to win support for ratification.
Patrick Henry led fierce opposition as Virginia narrowly ratified the Constitution in June 1788.
New York ratified the Constitution in July 1788, becoming the eleventh state to do so.
Port cities up and down the coast threw parades and feasts in 1788 to celebrate ratification.
George Washington was inaugurated as the first U.S. president in New York City on April 30, 1789.
The first Congress proposed the Bill of Rights, ten amendments guaranteeing basic liberties, in 1789.
The Judiciary Act of 1789 created the federal court system and the Supreme Court.
Congress passed its first tariff act in 1789, taxing imports including rum and molasses.
Washington formed his first cabinet in 1789, naming Hamilton to head the Treasury and Knox the War Department.
The first U.S. Census, taken in 1790, counted just under four million people.
The Naturalization Act of 1790 set the first federal rules for how immigrants could become citizens.
Samuel Hopkins received the first U.S. patent ever issued, for a potash-making process, in 1790.
Rhode Island became the last of the original states to ratify the Constitution, in May 1790.
Jefferson, Hamilton, and Madison struck a deal over dinner in 1790, trading the capital's site for debt relief.
The Bill of Rights was ratified on December 15, 1791, becoming the Constitution's first ten amendments.
Congress taxed whiskey in 1791 to pay war debts, infuriating frontier distillers.
Alexander Hamilton's First Bank of the United States was chartered in 1791.
Vermont joined the Union in March 1791 as the 14th state, the first added after the original 13.
Pierre Charles L'Enfant drew up the ambitious street plan for Washington, D.C., in 1791.
The Coinage Act of 1792 created the U.S. Mint and defined the dollar as the nation's currency.
Kentucky broke away from Virginia to become the 15th state in June 1792.
George Washington was unanimously re-elected president in 1792.
Twenty-four brokers signed the Buttonwood Agreement in 1792, founding what became the New York Stock Exchange.
Mint tradition holds that Martha Washington donated household silver to help strike the first U.S. coins in 1792.
Eli Whitney invented the cotton gin in 1793, reshaping the Southern economy around cotton.
Washington's Neutrality Proclamation of 1793 kept the U.S. out of the war between Britain and France.
A yellow fever epidemic tore through Philadelphia, then the nation's capital, in the summer of 1793.
George Washington laid the cornerstone of the U.S. Capitol building in September 1793.
French envoy Citizen Genet sparked a diplomatic crisis in 1793 by courting American support for France.
George Washington personally led militia troops to crush the Whiskey Rebellion in 1794.
The Battle of Fallen Timbers in August 1794 broke Native resistance in the Northwest Territory.
Congress passed the Neutrality Act of 1794, barring Americans from fighting for foreign powers.
The Jay Treaty, negotiated in 1794, resolved lingering disputes with Britain after the Revolution.
The Naval Act of 1794 authorized the first frigates of what became the U.S. Navy.
Pinckney's Treaty of 1795 won Americans the right to navigate the Mississippi River and use New Orleans.
The Senate ratified the controversial Jay Treaty in 1795 despite fierce public protest.
The Treaty of Greenville in 1795 ended the Northwest Indian War and opened much of Ohio to settlers.
The Eleventh Amendment, shielding states from many federal lawsuits, was ratified in February 1795.
Frontier settlement kept booming in 1795, swelling the population of the future state of Tennessee.
George Washington published his Farewell Address in 1796, declining to seek a third term.
Tennessee became the 16th state in June 1796.
John Adams won the 1796 election, the first genuinely contested U.S. presidential race.
Amelia Simmons published 'American Cookery' in 1796, the first cookbook written by an American.
The Supreme Court upheld a federal carriage tax in Hylton v. United States, its first constitutional case, in 1796.
John Adams was inaugurated as the second U.S. president in March 1797.
French agents demanded bribes from American envoys in the 1797 scandal known as the XYZ Affair.
USS Constitution, later nicknamed 'Old Ironsides,' was launched in Boston in October 1797.
Rising tensions with France in 1797 set the stage for an undeclared naval conflict, the Quasi-War.
Charles Newbold patented a cast-iron plow in 1797, though many farmers feared it would poison the soil.
Congress passed the Alien and Sedition Acts in 1798, cracking down on immigrants and press criticism.
The Quasi-War, an undeclared naval conflict with France, escalated through 1798.
Jefferson and Madison secretly wrote the Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions in 1798, defying federal law.
Congress reestablished the U.S. Marine Corps in July 1798.
A patriotic tune got new lyrics in 1798 as 'Hail, Columbia,' an early unofficial national anthem.
George Washington died at Mount Vernon on December 14, 1799, after a sudden throat illness.
Washington's Mount Vernon distillery was one of the largest whiskey producers in America by 1799.
Pennsylvania farmers staged a tax revolt known as Fries's Rebellion in 1799.
Aaron Burr founded the Bank of the Manhattan Company in 1799, cloaked as a water utility.
Washington's will, made public in 1799, ordered his enslaved people freed after Martha's death.
The federal government officially moved to the new capital, Washington, D.C., in 1800.
The 1800 election ended in an electoral tie between Jefferson and Burr, thrown to the House to decide.
Congress established the Library of Congress in April 1800.
John Adams became the first president to live in the executive mansion in November 1800.
The 1800 census counted about 5.3 million Americans, roughly a third more than a decade earlier.
